sections.title: What modals do (core logic)
sections.content:
Modal verbs (**can, could, may, might, must, shall, should, will, would**) add a layer of **speaker stance** to the main verb — necessity, ability, possibility, advice, permission. They tell the listener how the speaker sees the action.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: Modals are the modality dimension of the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model). Tenses locate events in time; modals colour them with the speaker's attitude. Without them, English sounds robotic — every workplace email, polite request, exam essay, and real conversation runs on them.

**Form — the three rules that never change**:
- **No -s** in the third person: *she can swim* (not *she cans*).
- **Base form** of the main verb follows: *I must go*, *you should see* (no *to*, no *-ing*, no past tense).
- **No do** in questions or negatives: *Can you help?* / *I can't help* (not *Do you can help?*).

**Czech note**: *můžu, musím, měl bych* often map to modals, but the form rules are different — Czech conjugates the modal (*musíš, musíme*), English does not.

sections.title: Ability & permission: can / could / be able to
sections.content:
**Ability** = what someone is capable of. **Permission** = what someone is allowed to do.

**Contrast examples**:
- *I **can** swim.* (present ability) vs *I **could** swim when I was six.* (past ability)
- *You **can** leave early.* (informal permission) vs ***May** I sit here?* (formal permission) vs ***Could** you help me?* (polite request)
- *She **will be able to** come tomorrow.* (future — *can* has no future form, so we switch to **be able to**)
- *He **has been able to** finish.* (perfect — same reason)

**Why it matters**: Asking for things politely is one of the most visible markers of fluent English. *Can I?* / *Could I?* / *May I?* is a gradient from casual to formal — students who only have *can* sound abrupt in work and exam contexts.

**Key gap**: *can* covers present only. The moment you need future, perfect, or *-ing*, you must use **be able to**.

sections.title: Necessity & obligation: must / have to / mustn't / don't have to
sections.content:
This is the highest-error area for Czech speakers. Four forms, four distinct meanings.

**Positive — must vs have to**:
- ***must*** = internal / speaker-decided obligation: *I **must** call my mother* (I feel I should).
- ***have to*** = external obligation (rule, boss, law): *I **have to** wear a uniform at work.*

**Negative — the critical trap**:
- ***mustn't*** = prohibition (don't do it!): *You **mustn't** smoke here.*
- ***don't have to*** = no obligation (you can if you want): *You **don't have to** come — it's optional.*

**Why it matters**: *mustn't* and *don't have to* look like opposites of the same thing but mean completely different things. Confusing them in a workplace email ("You mustn't reply" when you meant "you don't have to reply") changes the message entirely.

**Form note**: ***have to*** behaves like a normal verb — *She **has to** go. **Do** you **have to**? I **don't have to**.* It takes *do/does/did* in questions and negatives, unlike true modals.

**Czech trap**: *musím* maps to both *must* and *have to* depending on context. *Nemusím* is **don't have to** (no obligation), NOT *mustn't*.

sections.title: Advice: should / ought to / had better
sections.content:
Three forms on a gradient of **urgency**.

**Contrast examples**:
- *You **should** see a doctor.* (general advice — neutral, friendly)
- *You **ought to** apologise.* (similar to *should*, slightly more formal / moral)
- *You'**d better** leave now or you'll miss the train.* (urgent — warning of a bad consequence)

**Why it matters**: Giving advice is everywhere — to friends, colleagues, students, in writing tasks. The wrong level of urgency makes the speaker sound either bossy or weak. ***had better*** is much stronger than students realise — it implies a threat or warning, not gentle advice.

**Form notes**:
- ***ought to*** is the only modal that keeps *to*: *ought to go* (not *ought go*).
- ***had better*** + base form: *you'd better go* (not *had better to go*). Contraction *'d better* is normal.
- Negative of *should* is *shouldn't*; of *had better* is ***had better not***: *You'd better **not** be late.*

sections.title: Speculation & deduction — present: must / might / could / can't
sections.content:
Modals also express how **certain** the speaker is about something now. This is one of the most useful skills at B1+ — guessing, deducing, hedging.

**Contrast examples (degree of certainty)**:
- *She **must** be at home — her car's outside.* (almost sure — positive)
- *She **might / may / could** be at home — I'm not sure.* (possible — about 50/50)
- *She **can't** be at home — she's in Paris.* (almost sure — negative; NOT *mustn't*)

**Why it matters**: This is the heart of natural conversation — "He **must** be tired," "That **might** work," "She **can't** be serious." Exam speaking tasks (FCE/CAE) reward students who can hedge with *might/could* rather than asserting flat facts.

**Critical trap**: For negative deduction, the opposite of ***must*** (certain) is ***can't*** (certain not) — NOT ***mustn't***. *Mustn't* is prohibition, not deduction.

**Form**: All take **base form** of the verb: *must be, might rain, could work, can't know*.

sections.title: Speculation & deduction — past: must have / might have / can't have
sections.content:
Same logic as present deduction, but about something that already happened. **Modal + have + past participle**.

**Contrast examples**:
- *She **must have** left early — her coat's gone.* (almost sure she left)
- *He **might / may / could have** missed the train.* (possible)
- *They **can't have** finished already — it's only been an hour.* (almost sure they didn't)
- *I **should have** called her.* (regret — I didn't, but I wish I had)

**Why it matters**: This is the modality structure that separates B1 from B2 speakers. Native English uses it constantly to reason about past events ("He must have forgotten," "They could have warned us"). It's heavily tested in CAE/FCE Use of English (key word transformations).

**Form trap**: It's *must **have** done* — the *have* is required. In speech it contracts to *must've /ˈmʌstəv/*, which Czech learners often hear as *must of* and write incorrectly. It's always **have**.

sections.title: Past forms & common errors
sections.content:
Most modals have **no past form** — you switch to a different verb or structure.

**Past equivalents**:
- ***must*** → ***had to*** (past obligation): *I **had to** work late yesterday.* (NOT *I must work yesterday*)
- ***can*** → ***could*** / ***was able to***: *I **could** swim at five.* (general past ability) vs *I **was able to** finish the report on time.* (one specific success)
- ***should*** → ***should have + past participle*** (past regret): *I **should have called** her.*
- ***may/might*** → ***might have + past participle*** (past possibility): *He **might have forgotten**.*

**Top errors Czech speakers make**:
- *She cans swim.* → *She **can** swim.* (no *-s*)
- *You must to go.* → *You **must** go.* (no *to* after true modals)
- *I can to swim.* → *I **can** swim.* (no *to*)
- *Do you can help?* → ***Can** you help?* (no *do* with modals)
- *I should went.* → *I **should go** / I **should have gone**.* (modal + base form, always)
- *I must work yesterday.* → *I **had to** work yesterday.* (*must* has no past)
- *He must of forgotten.* → *He **must have** forgotten.* (always *have*, never *of*)

**Why it matters**: These six patterns cover the vast majority of modal errors in marked work. Fixing them gives an immediate, visible level-up in writing and speaking — and they're all checked in every Use of English paper.

For full modal reference, see **Reference** → **Modal Verbs**.

questions.question: What is **modality** in grammar?

options.a: The time of the action (past, present, future)
options.b: The way we express possibility, necessity, permission, advice, etc.
options.c: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject

questions.explanation: **Modality** is how we express meanings like possibility, necessity, permission, ability, obligation, or advice – the 'layer' of meaning added by modals and semi-modals.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a **semi-modal**?

options.b: You ought to see a doctor.

questions.explanation: **Ought to** is a semi-modal (followed by **to** + base verb). **Can**, **must**, and **might** are core modals (no *to*).

questions.question: 'You ____ apologise.' (strong advice, formal) Which fits best?
questions.explanation: **Ought to** expresses advice or moral obligation, often in a more formal tone. *Should* is also possible but *ought to* fits 'formal' best here.

questions.question: Which of these uses 'to' before the main verb? (e.g. ___ to go)
questions.explanation: **Ought to** always includes 'to' before the main verb: we say 'ought to go', 'ought to see'. **Must**, **should**, and **can** are followed directly by the base form (must go, should see, can go).

questions.question: For external obligation (e.g. a rule or someone else's requirement), we often use:
questions.explanation: **Have to** is commonly used for external obligation (rules, boss, law). **Must** can express the speaker's own obligation or a strong rule; **have to** is typical when the obligation comes from outside.

questions.question: Which verb do we use for **ability** when we need a form that *can* doesn't have (e.g. future)?
questions.explanation: **Be able to** is used for ability when we need another tense or structure, e.g. *She will be able to come*, *I have been able to finish*. **Can** has no future or perfect form.

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose the modal or semi-modal

questions.question: You ____ (advice) see a doctor if your headache continues. (ought to / should)
She ____ speak three languages. (ability)
questions.explanation: Ability in the present → **can** + base verb.

questions.question: We ____ wear a uniform at school. (external rule)
It ____ rain later, so take an umbrella. (possibility)
You ____ (not) tell anyone – it's a secret. (obligation not to)
questions.explanation: Obligation not to do something → **must not** or **cannot**.

questions.question: He ____ give up smoking. (advice, semi-modal)
questions.explanation: Advice with the semi-modal → **ought to** + base verb.
